summary: Justin Brennan leads a mentorship Q&A focused on troubleshooting the updated deal analyzer spreadsheet tool. The session covers closing costs allocation, equity raise calculations, sources and uses mechanics, and applies the analyzer to a real 32-unit multifamily deal in Kansas City, demonstrating how to identify which deal fundamentals (price, revenue, expenses) need adjustment to achieve target returns.
takeaways: When raising capital, overestimate the equity needed—if numbers show $500k needed, shoot for $600k instead to avoid under-capitalization
Closing costs are typically split 75% equity / 25% loan coverage; adjust loan-to-value assumptions accordingly
Total equity to raise includes purchase price portion + renovation dollars + uncovered closing costs, shown in 'sources and uses' section
For interest reserves when property won't cash flow during renovation, calculate annual debt service (e.g., $16,200/month × 12 months = ~$195k) and add as line item to total project cost
Deal fundamentals analysis: once revenue, expenses, and interest rates are locked in, only three levers remain—property price, rental rates, or down payment percentage
Cap rate arbitrage matters: if buying at 6.83 cap and selling at 6 cap, need minimum 2-point spread differential to create value
Bridge loans justified when DCR doesn't pencil on stabilized basis; allows rent growth before permanent financing
For refinance timing: year 3-4 refi may work only if significant value-add justifies new valuation; stable 2020 product better held 5-6 years without refi

skill_candidates.change: Use 'total equity' line from sources/uses (not just down payment %) when determining capital raise target
skill_candidates.evidence: Justin shows John's deal with $2.2M down payment but $2.4M total equity to raise, clarifying the difference includes renovation and closing costs; recommends overestimating by 10-20%

skill_candidates.change: Test deal sensitivity by isolating one variable at a time: lock revenue and expenses, then move price or down payment percentage to find breakeven
skill_candidates.evidence: Demonstrates John's deal: kept rents and expenses constant, dropped price from $2.6M to $2.4M to $2.35M to find acceptable IRR, then tested 30% vs 35% down impact on cash-on-cash

skill_candidates.change: Validate cap rate exit assumptions explicitly: calculate 'spread differential' (entry cap minus exit cap) and confirm ≥2 points to justify hold thesis
skill_candidates.evidence: Shows John's deal buying at 6.83 cap, improving to 7.99 cap, selling at 6 cap—spread of ~2 points confirms value creation reasonable; mentions buying at 25 cap requires either 1031 buyer or aggressive rent increases to $1,300+/month (vs current $1,195)

skill_candidates.change: Distinguish when to refi vs buy-and-hold: only refi if rents increase 15%+ post-renovation; otherwise hold 5-6 years to avoid negative cash flow
skill_candidates.evidence: John's 2020 stabilized property showed refi doesn't add value because rent growth already baked in; Justin recommends skipping refi, keeping property 5-6 years, then exit—'it's not really a value-add to refi'

title: Tuesday Mentorship Q&A - How to Fund Your First Deal_04-14-26 | Justin Brennan
